primary_diagnosis.name: Psoriatic arthritis (oligoarticular) — acute exacerbation with dactylitis and knee effusion

pathophysiology_summary: Psoriatic arthritis is a seronegative spondyloarthropathy in which immune-mediated inflammation targets not only the synovium but the entheses and the whole tenosynovial apparatus of a digit, producing dactylitis (uniform 'sausage' swelling of the entire finger from MCP to DIP) alongside an oligoarticular, characteristically ASYMMETRICAL pattern that can involve a small digit and a large weight-bearing joint simultaneously. It occurs on a background of cutaneous psoriasis and is associated with nail dystrophy (pitting, onycholysis) reflecting shared nail-bed/entheseal involvement. The acute flare here produces a warm knee effusion and an intensely painful immobile digit with a preceding constitutional prodrome of fatigue. The critical clinical task is to distinguish this inflammatory flare from acute SEPTIC arthritis, which destroys articular cartilage within days: septic arthritis is typically MONOarticular with a single fiery-hot joint, a high swinging fever and rigors, and systemic toxicity — features ABSENT here (asymmetrical two-joint involvement of distinct types, only low-grade warmth without high fever or rigors, and the psoriatic discriminators of dactylitis and nail pitting). Because a joint effusion cannot be palpated or aspirated remotely, septic arthritis cannot be definitively excluded by telehealth, so the correct pathway recognises the psoriatic flare while arranging prompt in-person joint assessment (examination +/- aspiration) and robust safety-netting. First-line symptomatic control of the flare is a potent anti-inflammatory (e.g. a COX-2-selective NSAID such as celecoxib) prescribed by the GP/prescriber, with rheumatology referral for definitive assessment and consideration of intra-articular corticosteroid or disease-modifying therapy. It is NOT an isolated gout attack (dactylitis and nail changes rather than focal podagra), reactive arthritis (no preceding GI/GU infection) or, on the negative acute screen, a septic joint — those were the mimics to be actively excluded.

differential.evidence_basis: Whole-finger 'sausage' dactylitis plus an asymmetrical large-joint effusion, nail pitting and established psoriasis is the classic oligoarticular psoriatic pattern.

A hot, swollen, exquisitely painful joint with any fever must prompt active screening for a septic joint (a surgical emergency that destroys cartilage in days); it cannot be excluded remotely without joint aspiration.
Crystal arthritis can cause an acutely hot swollen joint, but classically focal (e.g. first MTP podagra) rather than uniform whole-digit dactylitis with nail pitting.
Reactive arthritis can produce an asymmetrical oligoarthritis with dactylitis, but usually follows a GI or genitourinary infection — needs screening for a preceding trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI Doctor should recognise an asymmetrical inflammatory/psoriatic pattern AND keep septic arthritis explicitly on the list to be screened and safety-netted — neither leaping straight to emergency escalation nor dismissing it as a simple flare.

differential.evidence_basis: Uniform whole-finger dactylitis (DI-001), nail pitting (DI-002), simultaneous involvement of two distinct asymmetrical joint types (DI-003), established psoriasis (DI-004), and NO high fever/rigors/systemic toxicity (DI-005) with a warm but not spreading-red, not hour-by-hour-worsening knee (DI-006) — a psoriatic flare with a NEGATIVE acute septic screen.

Clinically downgraded by the asymmetrical two-joint (distinct-type) pattern, absence of a high spiking fever or rigors, and the psoriatic discriminators — but NOT definitively excludable via telehealth (no palpation, no aspiration), so it is retained as the mandatory safety-net trigger and the reason for prompt in-person joint assessment rather than pure self-care.

Uniform whole-digit dactylitis with nail pitting on a psoriatic background is not the focal monoarticular podagra pattern of gout; joint aspiration for crystals remains the outpatient discriminator if doubt persists.
No preceding gastrointestinal or genitourinary infection, and an established psoriatic skin history — the picture is better explained by psoriatic arthritis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a NEGATIVE acute septic screen the leading diagnosis is a psoriatic (spondyloarthritis) flare managed longitudinally in the outpatient setting. Escalating this baseline to ED/000 by ambulance because 'telehealth cannot palpate or aspirate the joint' is the over-triage error this case is built to catch — the joint examination +/- aspiration is arranged as a PROMPT in-person review, not an emergency ambulance transfer, unless a genuine septic trigger appears.

differential.diagnosis: Psoriatic arthritis (oligoarticular) — acute flare with dactylitis and knee effusion
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: recognise the psoriatic spondyloarthritis flare, arrange prompt in-person GP review (joint exam +/- aspiration and rheumatology referral), facilitate a prescription anti-inflammatory through the prescriber, advise elevation/rest/analgesia, and attach mandatory safety-netting for a genuine septic joint (fiery-red spreading hot joint, high fever with rigors, systemic toxicity).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final action is recognise + arrange prompt in-person joint assessment + refer + safety-net at T2. Joint aspiration, synovial fluid analysis, inflammatory markers and imaging are appropriate prompt outpatient/in-person investigations; they do not require emergency ambulance transport given the negative acute septic screen.

red_flags.description: Hot, swollen, tense joint — the possibility of septic arthritis in any acutely hot swollen joint
red_flags.clinical_significance: A warm swollen knee effusion cannot be assumed benign; septic arthritis destroys cartilage within days. Here the skin is not spreading fiery-red and the joint is not worsening hour-by-hour, and there is no systemic toxicity — the acute septic features are negative — but the joint still needs prompt in-person assessment because sepsis cannot be excluded remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A truly septic joint left as a home-managed 'flare' would be irreversibly damaged; conversely, treating every warm psoriatic effusion as a 000 emergency over-medicalises a benign flare.

red_flags.description: Dactylitis (uniform whole-finger 'sausage' swelling) with an immobile digit
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dactylitis is a hallmark of psoriatic/seronegative spondyloarthritis and strongly favours a psoriatic flare over gout or a simple septic monoarthritis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the dactylitis pattern risks mislabelling the presentation and either over-escalating or delaying the correct rheumatological pathway.

red_flags.description: Severe pain preventing use of the hand and weight-bearing on the leg, in someone living alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: The functional loss of the dominant hand plus the knee in a patient living alone creates a self-care/safety risk requiring support arrangements, and drives the need for effective analgesia and prompt review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ignoring the social/functional risk could leave the patient unable to feed or care for herself safely at home.

red_flags.description: High spiking fever with rigors / systemic toxicity (the systemic marker of a septic joint or sepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Only a low-grade sense of warmth without rigors, tachycardia or breathlessness — systemic sepsis is not present, which (with the asymmetrical two-joint pattern) clinically downgrades septic arthritis. Development of a high swinging fever with rigors would be a T5 (000) trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen for and safety-net systemic sepsis would miss a deteriorating septic joint / bacteraemia.

red_flags.description: A single monoarticular fiery-hot joint with a skin portal of entry (the classic septic-joint pattern)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The presentation is asymmetrical across two distinct joint types simultaneously with no obvious skin breach near the joints — atypical for a single septic joint. A new single fiery-hot rapidly worsening joint would re-raise septic arthritis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an evolving monoarticular septic joint would risk permanent cartilage destruction.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Septic-joint over-triage on the hot swollen knee
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being driven by the dramatic hot swollen joint, the excruciating pain and the telephone-triage framing ('rule out destructive septic arthritis before morning') into emergency ambulance escalation. The discriminators — asymmetrical involvement of two distinct joint types at once, dactylitis, nail pitting, established psoriasis, and the absence of a high spiking fever or rigors — identify a psoriatic flare; the correct response is prompt in-person joint assessment with safety-netting, not a default 000.

Reasoning 'I can't palpate or aspirate the joint remotely, so I must send her to ED by ambulance to rule out a septic joint.' This is the primary error the case tests: with a negative acute septic screen the examination +/- aspiration is arranged as a PROMPT in-person GP/urgent review, not an emergency ambulance transfer.

The opposite failure: telling the patient it is 'just a psoriasis flare, rest and take ibuprofen' with no in-person joint assessment, no rheumatology pathway and no safety-netting — missing the residual possibility of a septic joint and the self-care risk of someone living alone with a useless dominant hand.

Labelling an acutely hot swollen joint as gout by availability bias and missing the dactylitis and nail changes that point to psoriatic arthritis, sending the patient down the wrong management pathway.

Adopting the source note's low-dose naltrexone and zinc as telehealth prescriptions — they are not evidence-based first-line treatment for an acute psoriatic flare — or an AI Doctor autonomously initiating a disease-modifying drug or high-dose prescription NSAID without prescriber assessment and contraindication checks.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the finger to confirm uniform tenosynovial swelling MCP to DIP and its tenderness (in-person exam: profound uniform violaceous soft-tissue swelling, dactylitis)

Examination of the knee to confirm a fluctuant effusion, warmth and restricted terminal flexion, and to test ligament stability (in-person exam: moderate fluctuant warm effusion, stable cruciate and collateral ligaments)
Inspection for a skin portal of entry for infection near the joints (in-person exam: no obvious portal)
Measured vital signs without a device (in-person: BP 130/80, HR 88, Temp 37.3 C, RR 16, SpO2 99%)
Joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis (cell count, Gram stain, culture, crystals) — the definitive test to exclude septic arthritis and gout
Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) and blood cultures
Plain radiograph / ultrasound of the affected joints
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the psoriatic (spondyloarthritis) flare and manage at T2: arrange PROMPT in-person GP review (within 24-48 hours, brought to same-day if any septic feature appears) for joint examination +/- aspiration and rheumatology referral; facilitate a potent anti-inflammatory (e.g. celecoxib) prescribed by the GP/prescriber with contraindication checks; advise elevation of the hand and knee above heart level, relative rest, ice and adequate analgesia; arrange practical support given she lives alone and cannot cook; and give MANDATORY safety-netting to same-day ED / 000 for a fiery-red spreading hot joint, a high spiking fever with rigors, or systemic toxicity (possible septic arthritis / sepsis).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the psoriatic flare given the classic dactylitis, nail pitting, psoriatic background and asymmetrical pattern with a negative acute septic screen. The real risks are (1) OVER-action — defaulting to ED/000 by ambulance to 'rule out a septic joint' when the acute screen is negative and prompt in-person review suffices; and (2) UNDER-action — dismissing it as a self-managed flare with no in-person joint assessment, no rheumatology pathway and no septic-joint safety-netting.

risk_scores.tool_name: Bedside septic-arthritis risk appraisal (clinical, pending aspiration)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Low-to-intermediate clinical probability of a septic joint: against sepsis are the asymmetrical two-distinct-joint pattern, absence of high spiking fever or rigors, no systemic toxicity, dactylitis and nail pitting on a psoriatic background; not fully excludable without synovial fluid analysis.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a psoriatic flare as the working diagnosis while mandating prompt in-person joint assessment (examination +/- aspiration) rather than either emergency ambulance transfer or pure self-care.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Psoriatic arthritis with active dactylitis and peripheral arthritis is treated with NSAIDs and local corticosteroid injection for symptomatic control and disease-modifying therapy (conventional or biologic) for persistent disease, coordinated through rheumatology; dactylitis is a recognised domain requiring targeted assessment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology — the acutely hot/swollen joint
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Any acutely hot, swollen joint must be assessed for septic arthritis, which typically requires joint aspiration; an inflammatory flare in a patient with known spondyloarthritis is managed with anti-inflammatories and rheumatology referral once sepsis is excluded, with escalation for systemic features.
